sanskrit:
स्पर्शान्कृत्वा बहिर्बाह्यांश्चक्षुश्चैवान्तरे भ्रुवोः।
प्राणापानौ समौ कृत्वा नासाभ्यन्तरचारिणौ॥
यतेन्द्रियमनोबुद्धिर्मुनिर्मोक्षपरायणः।
विगतेच्छाभयक्रोधो यः सदा मुक्त एव सः॥
transliteration: sparśānkṛtvā bahirbāhyāṃścakṣuścaivāntare bhruvoḥ / prāṇāpānau samau kṛtvā nāsābhyantaracāriṇau // yatendriyamanobuddhirmunirmokṣaparāyaṇaḥ / vigaticchābhayakrodho yaḥ sadā mukta eva saḥ //
literal_meaning: Having shut out external contacts, keeping the eyes between the brows, equalizing the inward- and outward-going breaths within the nostrils, the sage whose senses, mind and intellect are controlled, whose goal is liberation, free from desire, fear and anger—he is ever free.
contextual_meaning: Krishna gives Arjuna a micro-manual for self-regulation: withdraw attention from sensory pull, anchor gaze and breath, balance the autonomic nervous system, and disarm the affective land-mines (desire, fear, rage) that keep the mind in threat-scan mode. The verse maps a somatic route to what psychology calls ‘flow without grasping’—a trait-like freedom rather than a fleeting state.
psychological_state: High vagal-tone, low amygdala reactivity; meta-cognitive monitoring without fusion with thoughts; trait-like freedom from reactive affects.
trigger_scenario: You’re doom-scrolling at 2 a.m., heart racing after a heated text exchange; you close the apps, sit up, exhale slowly through the nose, soften the gaze, and feel the charge drain from chest to belly—realizing you can choose not to fire back.
anchor_text: Still your senses, balance your breath, and meet each moment free of desire, fear, or rage.
